annotations.label: Complex plane

anchor.snippet: the complex plane is the plane formed by the complex numbers

annotations.note: The complex numbers are defined as the structure `Complex` (notation ℂ), the formal complex plane.

annotations.label: Addition adds like vectors

anchor.snippet: Under addition , they add like vectors

annotations.note: `Complex.equivRealProdAddHom : ℂ ≃+ ℝ × ℝ` shows addition is componentwise, i.e. like vector addition in ℝ².

annotations.label: Multiplication in polar coordinates

anchor.snippet: The multiplication of two complex numbers can be expressed more easily in polar coordinates

annotations.note: `arg_mul_coe_angle` gives arg(xy)=arg x+arg y (as angles) and `norm_mul` gives |xy|=|x||y|, i.e. polar-form multiplication.

annotations.label: Multiplication by modulus 1 is rotation

anchor.snippet: multiplication by a complex number of modulus 1 acts as a rotation

annotations.note: `Complex.rotation : Circle →* (ℂ ≃ₗᵢ[ℝ] ℂ)` with `rotation_apply a z = a * z` realizes multiplication by a unit-modulus number as a linear isometry (rotation).

annotations.label: Argand/Gauss plane

anchor.snippet: The complex plane is sometimes called the Argand plane or Gauss plane

annotations.note: This is a naming convention, not a mathematical statement to formalize.

annotations.label: Complex number real and imaginary parts

anchor.snippet: the complex numbers are customarily represented by the symbol z , which can be separated into its real ( x ) and imaginary ( y ) parts

annotations.note: `Complex.re` and `Complex.im` are the structure projections giving the real and imaginary parts.

annotations.label: Polar coordinate representation

anchor.snippet: the point ( x , y ) can also be represented in polar coordinates with

annotations.note: `Complex.polarCoord` is the homeomorphism to (r,θ) coordinates, and `norm_mul_exp_arg_mul_I` gives z = ‖z‖·exp(arg z·I).

annotations.label: Modulus and argument

anchor.snippet: Here | z | is the absolute value or modulus of the complex number z

annotations.note: The modulus is the norm ‖z‖ and the argument is `Complex.arg : ℂ → ℝ` (principal value in (-π,π]).

annotations.label: Argument is multi-valued

anchor.snippet: the argument of z is multi-valued, because the complex exponential function is periodic

annotations.note: The cited reason, periodicity `Complex.exp_periodic : Periodic exp (2πI)`, is formalized, but Mathlib's `arg` is the single-valued principal branch rather than a multi-valued object.

annotations.label: Euclidean vector space structure

anchor.snippet: the geometric view of the complex numbers is implicitly based on its structure of a Euclidean vector space of dimension 2

annotations.note: ℂ carries the instance `InnerProductSpace ℝ ℂ` and `finrank ℝ ℂ = 2`, making it a 2-dimensional Euclidean space.

annotations.label: Positive direction of contour integration

anchor.snippet: By convention the positive direction is counterclockwise.

annotations.note: `Complex.orientation` fixes the standard (counterclockwise) orientation of ℂ, but the contour-integration sign convention itself is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Complex functions: z-plane and w-plane

anchor.snippet: Here it is customary to speak of the domain of f ( z ) as lying in the z -plane

annotations.note: This is informal terminology for domain/codomain planes, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Complex plane notation

anchor.snippet: The complex plane is denoted as

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the notation ℂ for the type `Complex`, matching the standard notation.

annotations.label: Argand diagram

anchor.snippet: An Argand diagram is a geometric plot of complex numbers as points z = x + iy

annotations.note: An Argand diagram is a visualization, not a mathematical object Mathlib formalizes.

annotations.label: Lengths represent real numbers

anchor.snippet: The lengths of straight lines and curves in the complex plane represent real numbers

annotations.note: This is an interpretive remark about diagram conventions rather than a formal statement.

annotations.label: Argument is a real number

anchor.snippet: the argument (phase) of a complex number is a real number, not a physical angle

annotations.note: `Complex.arg : ℂ → ℝ` returns a real number, with `arg_mem_Ioc` placing it in (-π,π].

annotations.label: Stereographic projection correspondence

anchor.snippet: We can establish a one-to-one correspondence between the points on the surface of the sphere minus the north pole and the points in the complex plane

annotations.note: `stereographic` is the open partial homeomorphism from sphere-minus-pole to the orthogonal complement plane, generalizing the sphere↔ℂ correspondence.

annotations.label: Unit disk maps to southern hemisphere

anchor.snippet: that entire region ( | z | < 1 ) will be mapped onto the southern hemisphere

annotations.note: This specific image computation for the unit disk under stereographic projection is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extended complex plane

anchor.snippet: the complex plane plus the point at infinity, is known as the extended complex plane

annotations.note: The extended complex plane is `OnePoint ℂ`, the one-point compactification adding a single point at infinity.

annotations.label: Latitude lines become circles

anchor.snippet: The lines of latitude are all parallel to the equator, so they will become perfect circles centered on the origin

annotations.note: The image of latitude lines as origin-centered circles under stereographic projection is not formalized.

annotations.label: Any stereographic projection yields one point at infinity

anchor.snippet: Any stereographic projection of a sphere onto a plane will produce one

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `OnePoint (plane) ≃ₜ sphere`, i.e. the sphere is the plane plus one point at infinity, but not phrased as a property of stereographic projection.

annotations.label: Two-valued square root relationship

anchor.snippet: Consider the simple two-valued relationship

annotations.note: Mathlib has single-valued `Complex.cpow`/sqrt but does not model the two-valued square-root relation w²=z as such.

annotations.label: Square root traces half circle

anchor.snippet: as z moves all the way around the circle, w only traces out one-half of the circle

annotations.note: This monodromy observation about the square root tracing a half circle is not formalized.

annotations.label: Branch cut and branch point

anchor.snippet: This is commonly done by introducing a branch cut

annotations.note: `Complex.slitPlane` (ℂ minus the non-positive reals) is the standard branch-cut domain for log/sqrt, but 'branch point' is not defined as a general concept.

annotations.label: Sheets of the cut plane

anchor.snippet: We call these two copies of the complete cut plane sheets

annotations.note: The notion of sheets of a multi-valued function's cut plane is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Square root of z²−1 has two branch points

anchor.snippet: so g evidently has two branch points

annotations.note: The branch-point analysis of √(z²−1) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Meromorphic function

anchor.section: Restricting the domain of meromorphic functions
anchor.snippet: A meromorphic function is a complex function that is holomorphic and therefore analytic everywhere in its domain except at a finite, or countably infinite , number of points

annotations.note: `MeromorphicAt`/`MeromorphicOn` formalize meromorphic functions as locally a ratio that is holomorphic away from isolated points.

annotations.label: Poles of a meromorphic function

anchor.section: Restricting the domain of meromorphic functions
anchor.snippet: The points at which such a function cannot be defined are called the poles of the meromorphic function

annotations.note: Poles are captured by `MeromorphicAt.order < 0` (the function tends to infinity there), but Mathlib has no dedicated named 'pole' predicate.

annotations.label: Gamma function poles

anchor.section: Restricting the domain of meromorphic functions
anchor.snippet: has simple poles at 0, −1, −2, −3

annotations.note: `not_differentiableAt_Gamma_neg_nat` shows Γ fails to be differentiable at non-positive integers, and `tendsto_self_mul_Gamma_nhds_zero` gives the simple pole with residue 1 at 0.

annotations.label: Even function infinite series

anchor.snippet: it's clear that f ( z ) is an even function of z

annotations.note: This particular series example and its evenness are not formalized.

annotations.label: Continued fraction convergence region

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that f ( z ) converges to a finite value if z is not a negative real number

annotations.note: This specific continued-fraction convergence result is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riemann surface for the square root

anchor.section: Gluing the cut plane back together
anchor.snippet: It is also possible to "glue" those two sheets back together to form a single Riemann surface

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Riemann surface theory, so the square root's Riemann surface is not formalized.

annotations.label: Derivative holomorphic except at zero

anchor.section: Gluing the cut plane back together
anchor.snippet: the derivative of f exists and is finite everywhere on the Riemann surface, except when z = 0

annotations.note: This statement is about differentiability on the square root's Riemann surface, which Mathlib does not construct.

annotations.label: Riemann surface for square root of z²−1

anchor.section: Gluing the cut plane back together
anchor.snippet: We can verify that g is a single-valued function on this surface by tracing a circuit

annotations.note: The Riemann surface for √(z²−1) and single-valuedness on it are not formalized.

annotations.label: Both surfaces have genus one

anchor.section: Gluing the cut plane back together
anchor.snippet: both versions of this Riemann surface are equivalent – they are orientable two-dimensional surfaces of genus one

annotations.note: Mathlib has no genus theory for Riemann surfaces, so this is not formalized.

annotations.label: The s-plane

anchor.snippet: one use of the complex plane is known as the s-plane

annotations.note: The control-theory s-plane (Laplace-transform domain) is not a formalized concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nyquist stability criterion

anchor.snippet: Another related use of the complex plane is with the Nyquist stability criterion

annotations.note: The Nyquist stability criterion is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: The z-plane

anchor.snippet: The z -plane is a discrete-time version of the s -plane

annotations.note: The control-theory z-plane (Z-transform domain) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Squaring and norm-squared are quadratic forms

anchor.snippet: the squaring function z 2 and the norm-squared x 2 + y 2 are both quadratic forms

annotations.note: `Complex.normSq` defines the norm-squared map x²+y², but neither it nor the squaring map z² is bundled as a `QuadraticForm` in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complex plane as quotient ring

anchor.snippet: The complex plane of this article is the quotient ring

annotations.note: The ring isomorphism ℝ[X]/(X²+1) ≅ ℂ is not present in Mathlib (only `ℝ[I] = ⊤` exists, not the quotient construction).

annotations.label: Other two-dimensional real algebras

anchor.snippet: There are two other ideals that yield quotient rings that are two-dimensional real algebras

annotations.note: Dual numbers (ℝ[X]/(X²)) are formalized as `DualNumber`, but split-complex numbers (ℝ[X]/(X²−1)) and the quotient-ring framing are not.
